regions.note: Earthy, mushroom, red cherry, sous-bois. Restrained and intellectual. Cool continental climate with limestone soils means slow, late ripening and high acidity.
Cranberry, cola, baking spice, forest floor. The Willamette Valley's marine influence brings rain and moderate temperatures, similar to Burgundy but with slightly riper fruit expression.
Dark cherry, thyme, schist minerality. At 45 degrees south, extreme UV radiation and wide diurnal temperature swings create thicker skins and more concentrated fruit than any other Pinot region.
Ripe cherry, raspberry, vanilla, warm spice. Coastal fog is the hero. Without it, this would be too warm for Pinot. The fog burns off by midday, giving afternoon warmth and morning coolness.
Light red cherry, herbs, chalk. Often made as rose. The coolest expression of Pinot Noir, barely enough warmth to ripen, creating wines of extreme delicacy and transparency.
Flint, oyster shell, green apple, chalk. Zero oak. The Kimmeridgian limestone (ancient seabed fossils) is tasted as a chalky minerality. Cool climate preserves razor acidity.

Hazelnut, butter, lemon curd. Barrel-fermented in French oak with regular lees stirring (batonnage). Same grape as Chablis, same region (Burgundy), but oak aging and warmer mesoclimate create a fundamentally different wine.

Tropical fruit, butter, vanilla, toast. Full malolactic fermentation converts sharp malic acid to soft lactic acid (the butter). New French oak adds vanilla and toast. Warm climate ripens fruit fully.
As Blanc de Blancs: laser acidity, chalk, lemon zest, brioche. Chardonnay at its coldest limit. Grapes barely ripen, which is the point. The high acidity becomes the backbone of Champagne.
Cassis, cedar, graphite, tobacco. Always blended with Merlot and Cabernet Franc. Maritime climate means vintage variation matters enormously. Gravel soils drain water and radiate heat.
Blackcurrant, dark chocolate, vanilla. Consistent warm sun means Cabernet ripens fully every year. Higher alcohol (14.5%+), riper tannins, and generous new oak create an opulent, powerful style.
Blackcurrant, eucalyptus, mint, terra rossa. The eucalyptus-mint note is unique to Coonawarra. Famous red soil (terra rossa) over limestone creates excellent drainage.
Blackberry, green pepper, tobacco. The Andes create a rain shadow. Cool nights at altitude preserve acidity that hot days alone would destroy. Outstanding quality-to-price ratio in world Cabernet.

Black pepper, smoked meat, violet, olive. The cracked black pepper comes from rotundone, more pronounced in cooler climates. Granite soils on steep slopes add mineral complexity. This is called Syrah.

Blackberry jam, chocolate, licorice, mocha. Old vines, some planted 150+ years ago, predating phylloxera. Warm climate ripens fruit to extreme levels (15%+ ABV). Less pepper, more fruit. This is called Shiraz.

Dark fruit, fynbos, black pepper, smoke. A genuine midpoint between Rhone restraint and Barossa power. The maritime influence of two oceans brings cooling breezes that preserve freshness. Called Syrah here, not Shiraz.

Lime, slate, white flowers, petrol hint. Off-dry: fermentation stops naturally, leaving residual sugar balanced by razor acidity. 7-9% ABV. Steep slate slopes maximize sun exposure in this marginal climate.
Peach, ginger, petroleum, honey. Usually fermented to dryness (unlike Mosel). Fuller-bodied because the Vosges mountains create a rain shadow. Alsace is one of the driest, sunniest regions in France.
Lime juice, toast, lanolin. Always bone dry, no residual sugar. Screwcap sealed (Australia pioneered this for Riesling). Warm days but cool nights at 400m elevation. Ages magnificently 10-20 years.
Green apple, mineral, citrus blossom. Deep glacial lakes moderate brutal continental winters. Both dry and off-dry styles. America's most exciting cool-climate Riesling frontier, still being defined.
Tar, roses, dried cherry, truffle, leather. The great paradox: looks pale as Pinot Noir, hits you with tannin like Cabernet. Nebbiolo's thin skin gives little color but massive phenolic compounds.
Rose, red cherry, spice, white truffle. Same grape, adjacent commune, but lower elevation with slightly warmer exposure. More elegant and approachable younger. Barolo's graceful sibling.

Dried herbs, cranberry, iron, alpine flowers. Called 'Chiavennasca' here. Terraced vineyards at 300-700m in an alpine valley. Less sun, less ripeness, but mineral purity and herbal complexity are haunting. Dramatically undervalued.
Passion fruit, gooseberry, cut grass, grapefruit. Intensely aromatic. The thiol compounds are amplified by cool temperatures and UV intensity at this latitude. This style conquered the global market.

Flint, lemon, white flowers, chalk. Mineral-driven, restrained. Same grape as Marlborough, almost unrecognizable. Cool continental climate suppresses the tropical thiols. Kimmeridgian limestone gives gunflint-mineral character.
Citrus, beeswax, melon, oak spice. Often blended with Semillon and barrel-fermented in French oak. The oak and Semillon add weight, texture, and age-worthiness that single-varietal Sauvignon lacks.

Cherry, vanilla, leather, coconut, tobacco. The American oak barrel is the defining factor, giving coconut, dill, and vanilla notes. Traditional extended aging (Reserva, Gran Reserva) creates leathery, tobacco-laced complexity.

Blackberry, espresso, dark chocolate, cedar. Called 'Tinto Fino.' 200m higher altitude than Rioja = wider day-night temperature swings = more concentrated fruit. French oak preferred here: cedar and chocolate instead of coconut.
Inky black fruit, tar, iron, roasted herbs. Called 'Tinta de Toro.' Many vines are ungrafted (pre-phylloxera) because sandy soils resisted the pest. Maximum concentration, maximum power.

Raspberry, kirsch, garrigue, white pepper, lavender. Famous galets roules (large round stones) absorb heat during the day and radiate it at night. This is why alcohol regularly hits 15%+ but tannins stay soft.

Called 'Garnacha.' Black fruit, slate, licorice. Llicorella (laminated slate) soils on extreme steep slopes force vine roots deep for water. Priorat's Garnacha is darker, more tannic, and more mineral than Chateauneuf's.

Strawberry, raspberry, earth, chocolate. Old bush vines, some 80+ years, dry-farmed. The maritime influence from Gulf St Vincent brings afternoon sea breezes. Australia's Grenache renaissance, closer to Chateauneuf than heavy Shiraz.
Quince, honey, beeswax, chamomile. The vintage, not the winemaker, decides if the wine is dry, off-dry, or sweet. Cool years produce dry wines; warm years with botrytis produce luscious sweet wines.
Mineral, wet stone, smoke. Always bone dry, always austere. Schist soils (unlike Vouvray's tuffeau limestone) create a more angular, mineral wine. Needs 5-10 years to even begin opening.

Stone fruit, tropical, waxy texture, oak spice. Old bush vines, 40+ years, unirrigated. More fruit-forward and generous than Loire Chenin, but the waxy texture and acidity remain. South Africa's most exciting white category.
